title: ESP-FLY Mini Drone
description: For my Discovery Project, I built a fully functional mini drone powered by an ESP32-S3, combining custom PCB hardware, embedded firmware, and PID-based flight stabilization.
thumbnailAlt: ESP-FLY drone assembled in orange 3D-printed frame
imageAlt: Custom drone control PCB under soldering setup

sections.heading: Project Idea
sections.content: For my Discovery Project, I set out to build a fully functional mini drone powered by an ESP32 microcontroller. I have always been fascinated by drones and robotics, and I wanted to go beyond just flying one. I wanted to actually understand and build the hardware and software that makes it work. The project involved designing and assembling a custom PCB, soldering SMD components, wiring the ESP32, and writing firmware to control all four motors.

sections.heading: Project Progress
sections.content: Over the semester, I worked through this in stages. First I sourced and assembled the hardware: getting the custom PCB fabricated, placing and soldering SMD components like resistors, capacitors, and motor driver circuitry, and wiring the ESP32 to the board. Next I moved into firmware. I configured the ESP32 to communicate with the motor control path using PWM signals and got the motors to arm and respond to throttle commands. After that I integrated the IMU for attitude sensing and started tuning the PID loop for stabilization. At this point the drone is mostly complete. The hardware is built, the motors are functional, and the remaining work is final tuning for stable flight.

sections.heading: Successes and Failures
sections.content: My biggest challenge was getting the motor drive path to behave correctly at startup. The PWM initialization had to be in the right range and sequence, otherwise the motors would not spin. I spent a lot of time debugging signal timing and understanding that startup behavior in detail. On the success side, I was really proud when all four motors finally spun up reliably and responded to control input. Successfully soldering the SMD components without bridging pads was another major win for me, especially since this was my first time working at that scale.

sections.heading: ECE Skills Gained
sections.content: This project touched several core ECE skills. First was embedded systems programming: I used the ESP32 Arduino framework to implement PWM output, sensor reads, and real-time control logic. Second was circuit assembly and PCB familiarity: reading the schematic, matching footprints to placements, and soldering fine-pitch SMD parts. Third was communication interfaces: PWM for motor control and I2C for IMU communication. Finally, implementing a PID loop gave me real hands-on feedback control experience beyond what I had only seen in theory before.

sections.heading: Final Thoughts
sections.content: Overall, this project deepened my interest in embedded systems and robotics. Seeing hardware and software come together in a system that physically moves is extremely motivating to me. It confirmed that embedded and controls is the direction I want to keep pursuing. My next steps are to finish stable flight and then add wireless telemetry so I can tune PID gains in real time.

blocks.title: Project Idea
blocks.text: For my Discovery Project, I set out to build a fully functional mini drone powered by an ESP32 microcontroller. I have always been fascinated by drones and robotics, and I wanted to go beyond just flying one. I wanted to understand and build the hardware and software that actually makes it work.

blocks.title: Hardware Bring-Up
blocks.text: I designed around a custom PCB workflow, soldered SMD components by hand, wired the ESP32-S3, and iterated the board until power and control paths were reliable. This was my first time doing full SMD assembly at this scale, so getting clean joints without bridging was a huge milestone.
blocks.alt: Custom flight-control PCB held for soldering and inspection

blocks.title: Component Inventory
blocks.caption: Component inventory diagram used to map parts and quantities before assembly

blocks.title: Project Progress
blocks.text: Over the semester, I built this in stages: hardware assembly first, then firmware integration. I configured PWM output for motor commands, got reliable spin-up and throttle response, integrated the IMU for attitude sensing, and started PID tuning for stabilization. The hardware is complete and motors are functional, with final tuning still in progress.

blocks.title: System Architecture
blocks.alt: ESP32 drone system architecture block diagram
blocks.caption: System architecture: power, sensing, control, and motor-drive data flow

blocks.title: Successes and Failures
blocks.text: The hardest part was startup behavior in the motor drive path: PWM initialization had to be in the right sequence and range, or the motors would not spin. Debugging that timing took real iteration. My biggest win was getting all four motors to spin up reliably and respond consistently to control inputs.
blocks.alt: Assembled drone in 3D-printed frame during testing

blocks.title: ECE Skills Gained
blocks.text: This project gave me practical experience in embedded firmware, PCB/schematic reading, SMD soldering, PWM and I2C interfaces, and PID feedback control. It helped connect control theory and real hardware behavior in a way classes alone never could.

blocks.title: Final Thoughts
blocks.text: This project confirmed that embedded systems and controls is the direction I want to keep pursuing. Next I want stable untethered flight, then wireless telemetry so I can tune PID gains in real time while testing.
blocks.alt: ESP32 board connected to LiPo battery during bench testing

blocks.title: Video Demo
blocks.text: Current bench test footage is embedded directly below.

longDescription: ECE Discovery Project — a fully custom quadcopter designed from scratch, powered by an ESP32 microcontroller for flight control, PID stabilization, and real-time telemetry.

title: AI Powered College Matcher
description: A full-stack web app that matches students to colleges based on experience preferences, not just stats.
longDescription: EduAlign helps high school students find colleges that truly fit their lifestyle, not just their GPA and test scores. Through a guided 3-step intake flow, an AI engine scores and ranks colleges across dimensions like academic intensity, social life, career support, and campus culture — then visualizes the fit with interactive radar charts. Students can save schools to a personalized list, build financial plans, compare colleges side-by-side, and browse community reviews.
thumbnailAlt: EduAlign dashboard showing a student's college shortlist and recent activity
imageAlt: EduAlign dashboard showing a student's college shortlist and recent activity

sections.heading: Background & Motivation
sections.content: The college search process is often reduced to a numbers game — GPA cutoffs, SAT ranges, and acceptance rates. But students spend four years living on a campus, not just attending classes, and the experience fit matters just as much as the academic one. I built EduAlign to shift that framing: instead of filtering by stats alone, the app surfaces colleges that align with who a student is and what they want their college life to feel like. The tagline captures the goal exactly — find colleges that match your experience, not just your stats.

sections.heading: Approach & Methods
sections.content: The matching engine is built around a 3-step intake flow. Step one collects a student's academic profile: GPA, SAT score, intended major, home state, extracurriculars, preferred school size, and in-state tuition preference. Step two presents eight experience sliders — Academic Intensity, Social Life, Inclusivity, Career Support, Collaboration vs Competition, Mental Health Culture, Campus Safety, and Overall Satisfaction — which are auto-tuned based on the student's profile and can be manually adjusted. Step three captures campus vibe through a tag selection UI (options like 'Entrepreneurial', 'Arts & creative', 'Study abroad', 'Diverse community') plus a free-text field for anything else. The AI then scores colleges across all these dimensions and returns the top four matches with a numeric match percentage.

sections.heading: Implementation
sections.content: The frontend is built with React, featuring a multi-step wizard with smooth transitions, slider-based preference controls, and a results page with interactive radar charts that overlay the student's preferences against each college's ratings — making it immediately clear where the fit is strong and where trade-offs exist. The backend is a Python API that handles profile processing, preference auto-tuning logic, and the AI-powered matching and ranking pipeline. The app also includes a College Reviews section (structured pros/cons/advice-for-freshmen format), a Financial Planner, a Compare Colleges tool, and a My Colleges dashboard where students can categorize saves as Dream, Target, or Safety schools and track their full match history.

sections.heading: Results
sections.content: EduAlign produces personalized top-4 college matches with match scores and radar chart visualizations that make preference alignment immediately interpretable. The auto-tune feature — which pre-populates experience sliders based on a student's academic profile and extracurriculars — meaningfully reduces friction while still giving users full control to adjust. The My Colleges dashboard ties the whole experience together, letting students manage their list, financial plans, comparisons, and match history in one place. The app is fully deployed and accessible at edualign-nymh.onrender.com.

sections.heading: Lessons Learned
sections.content: Building EduAlign deepened my understanding of how to turn abstract user preferences into a concrete, explainable ranking system. The auto-tune feature taught me how to design systems that feel intelligent without feeling opaque — sliders start pre-filled but the UI makes clear that they're just a starting point, keeping the user in control. I also learned a lot about UI design for multi-step forms: each step needed to feel purposeful and not overwhelming, which required careful thought about what to ask when and how to communicate what the system is doing with the inputs.

sections.heading: Future Work
sections.content: Several features are natural next steps: expanding the college database, adding a richer financial aid breakdown in the Financial Planner, and incorporating real student review data at scale to improve the accuracy of college experience ratings. On the AI side, a conversational interface that lets students explore matches through dialogue — rather than a form — could make the experience feel even more personalized. I'd also like to add email alerts so students can be notified when deadlines approach for colleges on their shortlist.

title: Diabetes Progression Analysis
description: Machine learning project analyzing and predicting diabetes progression based on health indicators.
longDescription: Developed a predictive model for diabetes progression using real-world medical datasets. Implemented regression techniques to estimate disease progression over time based on health indicators such as BMI, blood pressure, and glucose levels.
thumbnailAlt: Predicted vs actual diabetes progression scatter plot
imageAlt: Predicted vs actual disease progression on the test set

sections.heading: Background & Motivation
sections.content: Diabetes is a widespread health condition affecting millions worldwide. Predicting disease progression helps clinicians tailor treatments and improve patient outcomes. This project was motivated by the need for interpretable, data-driven tools that can leverage standard health indicators—such as BMI, blood pressure, and glucose levels—to estimate how a patient's condition may evolve over time. Understanding these patterns is valuable for both healthcare research and practical clinical decision support.

sections.heading: Approach & Methods
sections.content: I used the sklearn diabetes benchmark (Efron et al.): 442 patients, 10 baseline features, and a one-year disease progression target. I built a tuned Random Forest regressor with exploratory analysis, feature importance, residual diagnostics, and 10-fold cross-validation. Open the full project page for charts and metrics.

sections.heading: Implementation
sections.content: The project was implemented in Python using scikit-learn, pandas, and matplotlib. The pipeline includes preprocessing, model training with depth and min-samples constraints to limit overfitting, and programmatic figures styled for clarity.

sections.heading: Results
sections.content: Cross-validated MAE is about 48 on a target scale of 25–346; held-out R² ≈ 0.26. Feature importance highlights S5 (blood serum measure) and BMI, with BP third. Residual analysis shows the model underestimates high-progression cases—important if this were a clinical tool. Full project page: predicted vs. actual, CV by fold, importances, top-feature trends, and residual plots.

sections.heading: Lessons Learned
sections.content: This project reinforced the importance of understanding domain context before applying machine learning. I learned to balance model complexity with interpretability, especially in healthcare applications where explainability matters. I also gained experience with the full ML workflow: from raw data to deployed insights. The experience has deepened my interest in applying computational methods to real-world problems with meaningful impact.

sections.heading: Future Work
sections.content: Future improvements could include incorporating time-series data if longitudinal measurements become available, exploring gradient boosting and uncertainty quantification, and validating the model on external datasets. Integration with a simple web interface for clinician use could make the tool more accessible.

blocks.title: Background & Motivation
blocks.text: Diabetes is a widespread health condition affecting millions worldwide. Predicting disease progression helps clinicians tailor treatments and improve patient outcomes. This project was motivated by the need for interpretable, data-driven tools that can leverage standard health indicators—such as BMI, blood pressure, and glucose levels—to estimate how a patient's condition may evolve over time. Understanding these patterns is valuable for both healthcare research and practical clinical decision support.

blocks.title: Approach & Methods
blocks.text: I used the diabetes dataset from the Efron et al. study, which contains baseline measurements and a quantitative measure of disease progression one year later. The workflow included data preprocessing to handle missing values and outliers, exploratory data analysis to understand feature distributions and correlations, and feature selection to identify the most predictive variables. I implemented several regression techniques including linear regression, ridge regression, and LASSO, comparing them using cross-validation and metrics such as MSE and R-squared.

blocks.title: Implementation
blocks.text: The project was implemented in Python using scikit-learn for modeling and pandas for data manipulation. Key challenges included choosing appropriate hyperparameters for regularization and ensuring that the model remained interpretable for domain experts. I used visualization libraries like matplotlib and seaborn to create plots that highlight feature importance and model predictions. The codebase is modular to allow easy experimentation with different algorithms and feature sets.

blocks.title: Results
blocks.text: The regression models achieved competitive performance in predicting disease progression. Feature importance analysis revealed that certain baseline measurements—particularly those related to blood serum and body composition—were most predictive. The visualizations helped communicate these findings clearly and demonstrated how different health indicators contribute to the predictions. The project produced a reproducible pipeline that could be extended to larger or different medical datasets.

blocks.title: Lessons Learned
blocks.text: This project reinforced the importance of understanding domain context before applying machine learning. I learned to balance model complexity with interpretability, especially in healthcare applications where explainability matters. I also gained experience with the full ML workflow: from raw data to deployed insights. The experience has deepened my interest in applying computational methods to real-world problems with meaningful impact.

blocks.title: Future Work
blocks.text: Future improvements could include incorporating time-series data if longitudinal measurements become available, exploring ensemble methods for robustness, and validating the model on external datasets. Integration with a simple web interface for clinician use could make the tool more accessible. There is also room to explore uncertainty quantification to provide confidence intervals around predictions.
